speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying, Sonia?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went home. I went home with-- with my friend to his house. And they had a beautiful apartment, because they were in the ghetto. They never left the ghetto. And they-- after the war-- they were-- they just chose wherever they want to live. And they took the most beautiful apartment in a very beautiful section of-- of Lodz. And that's where they lived. And after, when I got there to his house, it was-- and it just so happened that they, in the ghetto, they lived where my cousin Judy lived. My-- my cousin Judy lived on the second floor, and they lived downstairs. So you know, I knew-- and I knew them from-- from there. And I also knew them-- she was from my city. He was not from my city. He was from Piotrkow, but she was from my city. She was from Belchatow. So-- and she had her cousin with her. So I-- so we lived there. I stayed there with them. Anyway, a few-- about a week later, I-- I went to the Jewish-- you know, you had to go to the-- I-- every day I went to the Jewish gemeinde to look at the lists, you know, of who survived. And I was standing in line to look at the list, and somebody comes up and talks to me. And I was-- about a week after I came home, he comes up and talks to me. And he tells me that I look like his wife. And-- and he talks to me, you know. I stand in the line, and I look at the lists, you know, and I walk away. And I go back to my friend's house. And he walks with me all the time. And-- and I stop in front of the house where we live. And he wanted me to, you know, go with him to-- for-- to a restaurant or something. I said, no, no. I'm going home to my, you know, to my friend's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he walked with me till my friend's house. And-- and I told him, this is where my friends live. And I said goodbye, you know? And I went into my friend's house, and he-- and he went away. And the next day, I was sitting in my friend's house, you know? And somebody comes to the door. You know, I was sitting in the living room on the couch. And somebody comes to the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my friend goes to open the door. And somehow, I don't even know if I told this man my name, but apparently, I did, or he saw it, or whatever. I don't know. And he asked if I lived here. And they say yes. And they come in. They come in. My-- my girlfriend comes in. And she says, [NON-ENGLISH], there's a-- a-- a gentleman, and he is asking if you live here. I said, I don't know anybody. I didn't meet anybody. I-- you know. And-- and, you know, and I ran to see who it is. And it was my man that I later married after two months. From this time on, he just wouldn't leave me. He lived-- at that time he didn't live in-- in Lodz. He lived in Opoczno, which was quite, you know-- which-- which was a, you know, like a smaller town. And he wouldn't leave my side. And I really wasn't ready to get married. I didn't want to get married, because it was just two months after the war. And, you know, and he was so good to me. He bought me clothes. He bought me this. And my-- at that-- by that time, my cousin-- my cousin, the one that was in the ghetto, she came back with her husband. They survived, and she came back. And my friend's house-- you know, we found out that when I went to look at the list, I found them, that they're alive. And I left-- I left a-- you know, my name, where I am. So they came to my friend's house, so-- my cousin. So-- and Mr-- at that time, was name was Mr. Symsnovich. He came. You know, he came and stayed at-- with-- in my friend's house all the time. I can't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he asked me to come with him to Opoczno, but I wouldn't go, you know. So my cousin, Judy, she says, I'll go with you, you know? I said, I'm not gonna go to a strange man's house, you know? So she says, I'll go with you, you know? And he-- he lived in Opoczno. And he was hidden with a gentile family. And he was liberated in January.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back to his city. And his parents were very rich people. And they were-- you know, when he got-- he was married before. He lost his wife and two children. And when he got married before, his parents gave him the-- there was a gas station-- the only gas station in town. They gave him, as a wedding present. And this is what he did-- had the gas station. And apparently, when he came home, he sold some of the property that his parents had. And he-- they also had a soap factory. And my-- he sold-- he sold the gas station. And the first money he got when he poured water into the-- into the gas station where the pumps were down there, so-- and the-- and the-- the gasoline came up. You know, they poured water in so the gas would come up. And the first gas that they pumped out, this was his first money that he got after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he sold some property that belonged to his parents. And he lived in the house that he used to live before. And we-- my cousin came with me, you know? And he was a, you know, he-- at that time, he already established himself. And he had like a little soap factory in Opoczno.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were, I don't know, maybe 10 people that survived-- some hidden-- most of them hidden-- that survived from Opoczno. And they were all-- were like a small little community living together. And when I got there, you know, there-- there were women that, you know, that didn't-- that didn't have husbands. There was a couple that survived with a son. And their name was Frankel; they were hidden-- and a sister. One of his sisters survived, and she thought that she's gonna marry my husband. So when I got there, they didn't like it. [LAUGHS] You know, they were all-- you know, all jealous and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I got there with my cousin. And he was established. And I-- I just didn't like it there. And so he says, well, if you don't like it here, fine. We'll live in Lodz, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and also, a lot of things happened while we were there before we got married. Some people came back, you now, like from the next town. There was one guy, a friend of my husband's, that came back. He survived the war. I don't know where, but he survived. And he was a dentist. And he had his, you know, his equipment and everything in this other small town. And he stayed one night, overnight, with us. And the next day, he went there to, you know-- he said he's gonna-- just wants to take his thing and his equipment if-- if it's still there, and go and establish himself in Lodz as a dentist. This man never got back. We found him a few days later in a ditch-- dead. And after this, I said, I'm, you know-- I-- I'm-- I'm not-- I don't want-- I'm not gonna live here. I'm gonna stay here. And the next day, we went back to Lodz. Well, we went back to Lodz, and I stayed with my friends. And my husband kept coming every few-- he wasn't my husband at that time-- kept coming for every weekend and everything. And he says, well, we just made up that he wants to marry me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all-- you know, at that time, my cousin said, you know-- my cousin and everybody said, look. Why don't you want to marry this man? You don't have nobody. You are nobody. Your house is burned down. Your-- everybody is dead. What are you? You know, you cannot live all the time with, you know, with these people. I really didn't want to get married yet, but my cousin, and my friends, and-- I figured to myself, why did I survive? Maybe that was the purpose, that I survived-- to maybe make this man happy. He lost his wife. He lost his children. So maybe I was left to make this man happy. And I said, OK. And we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't love him when I got married. But later on, he was so good to me. He was so-- such a wonderful person. He could go out in the street and make a friend just like this. And I-- I just learned to love this man so much that when he died, part of me died with me-- with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Anyway, we lived in Lodz. We got married after two months, in-- in the 25th of June, in-- in-- in the 25th of June, '45. We got married in my girlfriend's house. And my husband had a-- a cousin. He was a Polish Jew, but he was in the Russian army. And he was in Lodz. This, you know-- and he was like in the Russian administration of Lodz. But he was, you know, he was hidden. He didn't admit that he is a Jew, because at his high position-- wouldn't allow it. So-- but he was-- so he came to the wedding. And he was, at that time, he was married. And his wife was a beautiful blonde woman. I don't know if she was Jewish or not. And some, you know, friends that we made, some of my husband's friends that lived in Opoczno, that survived, they came. And there were about 50 people. And we got married. We got married in my girlfriend's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after-- after we had the chuppah, I, you know, I went into, you know. They took us into the bedroom, you know? And I couldn't stop crying for hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought to myself, I got married-- no mother, no father, nobody-- all by myself. I married this man that, you know, I didn't even love at the time. But I felt that maybe that was the purpose that I survived. I couldn't go out. You know, I just stayed there in the bedroom and cried. And my-- my friends and my cousins, everybody came in-- control yourself! You know, come out of this room. You know, they wiped my eyes and put my makeup. And, you know-- again I started crying. You know, it was just-- this went on for hours. Anyway, I finally pulled myself together. In the meantime, they had, outside they had a party, you know in there. My cousin prepared, and my friends-- you know, it was-- then we got married. And we-- I didn't-- we didn't go back to Opoczno. We stayed with my friends. My husband, since he didn't go back to Opoczno, he-- there-- and we lived on Pomorska. Your-- your father will probably know the address-- on Pomorska 39-- 41. It was a gorgeous building. It was like a closed-in building. And next-- and next door was like a-- was a house with a empty lot. So my husband bought the house. And in the back, he built his soap factory. And he took my friend in as a partner. And he started producing soap the same as his company was before the war-- parents' company before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while he was, you know-- and at that time, there was very little soap and very little thing being produced, you know. It was right after the war. And this company was very well-known before the war. And it became such a success. A lot of people that were here-- do you know Charlie Newman? Charlie Newman used to buy soap from my husband and sell it on the market, that he-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it became very successful. And there were a lot of soap that was made in little-- in-- in homes, you know-- homes. Like the Poles used to make-- like for-- while my husband was hidden, he also taught this gentile man to make soap. You know, he made it in like little batches. That's why he kept him. And he sold it. And-- and he-- that's, you know. And-- so afterwards, there were a lot of little-- in the-- in the villages, that were making in like, you know, like in the houses, in their little-- in barns. And they made soap. So when this came out on a bigger scale, the others you know, couldn't sell their product.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after awhile, we started getting threatening letters from the [NON-ENGLISH], you know? Like Friday-- Friday night, I would light candles, you know? And I would hear a knock at the door. And I'd go open the door. And there's a letter with a bullet inside. This was in Lodz, after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on for months. One time, there was a rumor that a man-- the wife of a soap manufacturer got killed in Lodz. Everybody thought it was me. All the people that knew that, because not everybody knew that we're getting threatening letters. Because you don't want to advertise this. And they-- they-- everybody came around. They thought that I was the one that got killed. It turned out another manufacturer from soap, that they were-- they killed the wife. Anyway, my-- they-- and with the letters, they said that he should write-- give them the recipe-- how to make soap the way this one is. So my husband would write-- and, you know, and make like a post office, you know, to write back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he would write back and tell them, whoever you are, please come. And I am-- I'll be only too happy to let you watch and teach you. It's not something that I can give you a recipe, like for a cake. Anyway, this went on for months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the meantime, mine-- my husband's cousin, you know, he knew everything what's going on. And they started, you know-- they gave my husband a gun. He went around with a gun. Every time we went out, he was with a gun. And I got sick and tired. And, you know, we lived on the second floor. And I got sick and tired of living like this-- being scared. Whenever we went into the building, we looked on all sides if there was somebody not waiting to kill my husband. And he went around with a gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told him, the same thing-- that I didn't want to be in Opoczno. I don't want to live this way either. And we are gonna leave. So my-- my-- you know, my friends-- so when we told them we're gonna leave-- so they wouldn't wanna-- didn't want us to leave. And by that time, the Russians, you know, sent, let's say, people to stay overnight with us. So we would be in the-- asleep in the bedroom, and they would sit outside with a bottle of vodka and drink a whole night. In the morning, he would leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I cannot live like this. And I don't want to live like this. And we want to leave. And we told my cousin, you know, that we want to leave-- I mean, my husband's cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, OK. I have to arrange it. You know, you cannot just pick yourself up and go. So he-- it took weeks, and weeks, and weeks. So we got away from the house where we were. And we got, you know, we got to-- and we had to get away from the-- the Russian [NON-ENGLISH], you know, because they were looking for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, they came to-- to guard us, and we were not there anymore. And they were mad. We didn't trust them. We didn't trust their-- you know, they were watching over us, and we didn't trust them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got to one of my husband's cousins, the one that-- in Brazil. So we got to them. And we stayed there for three weeks. In the meantime, they were looking for us everywhere-- all the train stations, all the bus stations, all the highways-- for three weeks, till everything calmed down. And by that time, my-- my husband's cousin was able to make arrangements for us to leave. We left with a-- with a-- [NON-ENGLISH] a rucksack? Ugh! How do you say that? A rucksack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, how do you say it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: A rucksack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Anyway, that's what we left. By that time, we had, you know, quite a bit of money. And, you know, my husband gave the factory, to that-- you know, wrote it over to this gentile man that hid him. And, you know, and he-- and he-- and he knew how to work it. So he left it to him, and the house that was in front. He left it to him, and we left to my cousin's-- cousin's house. We stayed there for three weeks till they arranged for transportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he send one Russian soldier, and I, as his wife, and we went to Berlin. My husband was in a different wagon-- in a different-- yeah-- and in a different part of the train. And he was like as a passenger. They gave him-- made him papers. And all that we had was in that [NON-ENGLISH]. Rucksack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No, that-- how do you say it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a backpack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Backpack, yeah. Everything we had-- all the money, you know, whatever it was in the-- mine change of clothes and things like this-- was all in that backpack. And the train stopped. We arrived in Berlin. The train stopped. And this Russian soldier-- by then, I spoke a little Russian, because Polish and Russian is very similar. And by that time, this was like the end of December, so I was a few months, you know, with the Russian. So I learned how to speak a little Russian, because it's very similar to Polish. And he was a gentleman all the time in the-- in the train. And everything was very, you know, about my welfare, and, you know, if I slept a little, and if I did this, and brought me this, and brought me that. Going off the train-- so I picked up the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Backpack.

speaker: interviewee
text: --backpack. And he said, oh, let me carry it. You know? And I practically pulled it out of my hand. And we get off the train, and I look around. You know, I didn't look for him. I looked for my husband-- if he got off the train. Because all this time I didn't know. Maybe they stopped someplace and they took him off, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I saw, you know, my husband getting off from the train from the other thing. And I look around. This guy is gone. So all we had was the clothes on my-- what I wore and what my husband wore. At that time, this is how we came to Berlin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the Jewish-- there was already like a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. And they took us to-- they had like a place where to stay for people that came, you know, from Poland or from wherever they came, you know. And so we stayed in Berlin, I think for about three weeks till we-- on a-- on a coal wagon-- on a coal train we, you know. One day we went to Munich. And we got into Munich. It was at the end of December. I think it was just after Christmas. And at that time, my cousin was already there. And that's how we got to--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States, we came in 1951. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: My son was born. We lived for about six years in Germany, and my son was born there in 1946. And in Germany, we-- [SIGHS] I got pregnant right away. Is this-- is this-- you want to hear about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got pregnant after six months. And my husband was already worried that I wouldn't be able to get pregnant, because, you know, he wanted me instantly to get pregnant. He lost his children, and he want-- he was very desperate to have another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after six months, I got pregnant. And we lived in Germany in one room. And I was fine while I was pregnant. I, you know, did everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a room. And I washed. And before the-- I went to the hospital, you know, when I was in the ninth month, I, you know, my husband and I, we painted the room and made everything ready. And-- because you couldn't buy any clothes. When the baby was born, they gave you a-- gave you a ration cards to go to a place and buy things for the baby. But before, they didn't give you anything. So we, you know, I remember I washed the windows and made everything ready. Anyway, after my son-- after my son was born-- after a week, I-- yeah-- part of my ninth month, I got-- my feet started swelling. And I got very sick. I had uremic poisoning. And I had to be in the hospital the whole ninth month. Well, in the hospital, a girlfriend of mine that we were very close friends-- and she was pregnant. And she was a month-- she gave birth a month before I was due. And her baby was born dead. And this was such a-- and I was in the hospital at that time. And this was such a terrible blow to me. I just couldn't-- I just couldn't-- you know, I was scared to death. And I, you know, they had to induce labor because of the condition that I was in. They induced labor, and my son was born. I didn't scream, because I was in the hospital the whole ninth month, and I-- whenever I went to take a bath, I-- they took me next to the labor room. And I heard all these women giving birth and screaming. You know, it was horrible! And I'd think to myself, you know, this is-- you know-- I don't know. [LAUGHS] And I thought to myself, I'm not gonna do that. And I-- I just moaned, and-- and groaned, and bit my lips, but I didn't scream. And after I gave birth, at that time they didn't have doctors. They had midwives. So the midwife came-- came over to me, and she kissed me. She says, the first time that she sees somebody giving birth without screaming. But, you know, I just couldn't stand all these screams. But it took me maybe four weeks till my lips healed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then your family, you all came to the United States. And you settled where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you made a living for yourselves here. And you lived with your husband and your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the beginning, my-- we-- my husband worked for a-- a chemical company-- took, you know, send us papers to come to the United States. And they employed my husband for a certain amount of time. But my husband was not being used to be employed by somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- we just went in business for ourselves. And in the beginning, we lost money. And then we went in-- with a friend of ours. We lended money from a friend of ours. And we also got the money from the [NON-ENGLISH]. So we went into business. And what happened to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband got sick in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --I think in the beginning of 1960. And at that time, we lived in Alhambra. And we had a store in South Pasadena on-- on Huntington Drive. And he got sick, and he couldn't eat. He started losing weight. And we went to doctors over there, and to all kinds of doctors. And they couldn't find out what was wrong with him. They said it's psychological. And they send him to a psychologist in, you know, Pasadena.

speaker: interviewer
text: He didn't want to go, but I made him go. He went once, and he says, what-- [LAUGHS] what do they-- what do they want? They ask me how our life is, how this is, how that is, how my-- what my childhood was like, and all these things. He says, what has this got to do with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to eat. I want to live. I don't want to lose weight. And--
